data.title: Decoding The Hardware-Software Performance Dispersion
data.description:
By Mark Barnes, PhD, Head of Global Investment Research, Americas, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell | Indhu Raghavan, CFA, Manager, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell | Belle Chang, MBA, Senior Manager, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell

  • The evolving AI narrative has shifted technology leadership from software to hardware. Hardware companies benefit from tangible demand, secured orders, and near-term cash flows, while software faces uncertainty around model economics, disruption risks, and capex payoff.
  • Recent regional equity dispersion reflects differing exposures within the AI theme. Hardware and Telecom Equipment weights explain why APAC ex Japan ex China and Japan have led global returns, while software-heavy regions, such as the US and China, have lagged.
  • In APAC, Japan, Korea and Taiwan outperformed due to their heavy weights in Hardware and Telecom Equipment. These markets benefit from robust demand for memory chips, advanced semiconductors and precision equipment, essential for AI supply chain and infrastructure.

Introduction

Global equity markets have been driven to a great extent by the AI capex-fueled tech rally over the last three years. Until recently, there was not much differentiation between the Hardware and Software sectors of the Tech industry. Exhibit 1 shows that the two sectors diverged a bit in 2024, but by April 2025, when the US had announced and paused reciprocal tariffs, the FTSE All-World Software and Hardware sectors had almost identical returns for the period 31 March 2023 – 8 April 2025 (37% vs 35%). However, soon after, the two sectors began to rapidly diverge, with Hardware outperforming Software by 171% to 72% by February 2026 for the full period. This differentiation is due to a maturing of the AI narrative and has interesting implications for returns within technology and related sectors globally.

Exhibit 1: FTSE All-World Software and Hardware sector cumulative returns (USD, rebased)

Evolution of the AI narrative

The Hardware-Software performance divergence is due to fundamental differences in the relationship of the two sectors to the AI narrative, uncertainty over the path of AI development, and more nuanced differentiation recently within the software component. The Software sector has been core to the AI narrative because that is where the foundational AI model providers, such as Google (GOOGL) and Meta (META) (besides a host of smaller and private companies), sit. On the other hand, hardware companies have played a supporting role providing the hardware necessary to develop and distribute these models, with the prime example being Nvidia (NVDA). In the early excitement, the AI tide lifted all boats, and both sectors did well.

However, in January 2025, the release of China’s relatively cheap (and high quality) DeepSeek model left investors questioning whether the massive investments in LLM development were really necessary, and whether over-investment in AI could leave investors undercompensated. The difference here is that the payoff to AI software providers was to come at some undetermined time in the future while the support staff in the Hardware sector had contracts, were being paid, and were generating earnings right now.

Finally, in early 2026 we saw a differentiation within the software sector as new releases of LLMs showed the promise of AI tools easily replacing legacy software. Investors reacted energetically, generating sizable dispersion between the performance of those expected to be disruptors vs. 'disruptees'.

Regional differences

One aspect of how this divergence can impact global equity markets is through the relative weights of Tech sectors in different regions, in addition to related sectors such as Telecommunications Equipment that is an important component of the AI support group providing connectivity hardware. Exhibit 2 shows that there are important regional differences between Software, Hardware, and Hardware and Telecom Equipment combined. Software weights are much higher in the FTSE USA and FTSE China indices compared to others. Hardware is similarly large in FTSE USA but simply dominates FTSE All-World Asia Pacific ex Japan ex China and the FTSE Japan indices compared to their Software weights. Finally, including Telecom Equipment is only important in Asia Pacific ex Japan ex China, where that combined group accounts for over a third of index weights.

Exhibit 2: Sector weights in regional FTSE indices

The recent differentiation within the AI theme can be seen in sector level performance in different regions. Exhibit 3 shows the cumulative returns for the Software and Hardware sectors in different regions during June 2025 and February 2026, approximately since the start of Hardware’s outperformance of Software at the global level. We observe the same trend across regions. In addition, the Telecom Equipment sector of the Asia Pacific ex Japan ex China index returned a whopping 254% over that time period.

Exhibit 3: Regional Software versus Hardware sector performance (USD, June 2025-February 2026)

If we combine the impacts of index exposure to AI-related sectors and the recent performance of those sectors, we can see that one of the major drivers of regional equity return dispersion recently is the AI-related performance differentiation.

Exhibit 4 shows the cumulative returns for the last 12 months ending February 2026. During this period we have seen a number of shocks related to US tariffs, monetary policy divergence and election shocks. However, it is interesting that among the worst-performing indices are the US and China, which have the largest Software sector, while the best-performing regions have been Japan and Asia Pacific ex Japan ex China, which has the largest Hardware + Telecom Equipment sector group. We take a closer look at these regions in the next section.

Exhibit 4: Cumulative returns of regional FTSE indices (USD, rebased)

The hardware-heavy APAC tech

As shown in Exhibit 2, Asia Pacific (APAC) markets, with the notable exception of China, have higher weights in Hardware and Telecom Equipment stocks, contributing to the recent outperformance of APAC vs the US or Europe.

If we take a closer look at individual markets’ sector breakdown (Exhibit 5), China and India have higher weights in Software, while Taiwan, Korea and Japan are more concentrated in Hardware, particularly Semiconductors, and Telecom Equipment. The region’s distinct industry structures and competitive advantages riding AI waves explain why, over the past year, Korea, Japan and Taiwan have outperformed China and India in tech (Exhibit 6).

EXHIBIT 5: TECH SOFTWARE VS HARDWARE AND TELECOM SECTOR WEIGHT OF MAJOR APAC MARKETS (% OF FTSE ALL-WORLD ASIA PACIFIC INDEX)

Weight (% of All-World Asia Pacific Index) Taiwan China Korea Japan India Sum
Software Computer Services 0.12% 0.05% 0.57% 1.00% 1.74%
Software 0.15% 0.07% 0.02% 0.24%
Consumer Digital Services 4.32% 0.36% 0.15% 0.11% 4.94%
Hardware Semiconductors 8.20% 0.21% 1.47% 0.29% 10.17%
Electronic Components 1.04% 0.21% 0.14% 0.51% 0.01% 1.91%
Production Technology Equipment 0.21% 0.05% 0.02% 1.51% 1.79%
Computer Hardware 0.76% 0.02% 0.01% 0.27% 1.07%
Electronic Office Equipment 0.38% 0.38%
Telecom equipment Telecommunications Equipment 0.12% 0.73% 2.64% 3.50%
Software + Hardware + Telecom Equipment (% weight of country/market) 81.12% 60.54% 28.36% 14.89% 11.75%

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. Data as of 28 February 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

In Korea, the strength of the hardware segment is anchored by the explosive demand for memory chips produced by SK Hynix (HXSC.F). Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), classified as Telecommunications Equipment under ICB, not only delivered strong growth in smartphone sales, but also has benefited from securing orders of memory chips from Nvidia. Taiwan’s hardware outperformance is tied to its dominance in advanced semiconductor manufacturing. Japan’s gains come from its precision equipment makers, who supply the specialized tools and machinery essential for producing cutting‑edge semiconductors.

EXHIBIT 6: 1-YEAR TOTAL RETURNS (USD, %)

Sector Sub-sector Taiwan China Korea Japan India APAC All-World
Software Computer Services 8% 81% 8% -21% -6% -10%
Software 2% -42% -15% -23% -10%
Consumer Digital Services -2% 79% -8% -5% 2% 33%
Hardware Semiconductors 96% 74% 471% 59% 120% 64%
Electronic Components 76% 52% 203% 71% 83% 96%
Production Technology Equipment 162% 81% 255% 134% 136% 132%
Computer Hardware 30% 14% 59% 35% 28% 15%
Electronic Office Equipment 0.10% 0.10% 0.10%
Telecom Equipment Telecommunications Equipment 111% -13% 303% 173% 91%

Source: FTSE Russell/LSEG. Data as of 28 February 2026. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

In contrast, the uncertainty around US-India tariffs and US H1-B visa weighed on Indian software stocks in 2025. In China, software stocks lagged hardware stocks. Chinese financial regulators’ cautious stance on bull markets has cooled industries, including the software sector, which saw strong returns after the DeepSeek AI boost in January 2025. Concerns on valuation and profitability also weighed on software stocks. However, as discussed in China equity: What to watch after the fourth plenum? (Dec 2025), hardware stocks continued to benefit from rising chip demand, and the government’s push on tech self-reliance and anti-involution campaigns.

Conclusion

As the AI narrative has evolved, we have seen differentiated performance between various pockets of the AI ecosystem such as the Software, Hardware and Telecom Equipment sectors to name a few. Regional equity indices’ relative exposure to these sectors have been one source of recent regional equity return dispersion. Hardware-heavy regional industries and equity indices, such as those in APAC, have benefitted enormously from Hardware’s outperformance of Software. To the extent that this trend continues, we may potentially see further return dispersion as a result.

data.title: Integra Resources: A Softer Q4, But A Very Compelling Valuation
data.description:
Overview

Integra Resources (ITRG) (ITR:CA) is a gold mining company listed in both the United States and Canada. The reporting currency is U.S. dollars. The company has three primary assets in the United States: Florida Canyon, DeLamar, and Nevada North. Florida Canyon is a producing mine, and the latter two are development projects, where DeLamar is further along the development cycle.

I have covered Integra several times over the last few years, and my prior articles can be found here. Today's article will focus on the Q4 2025 result that was released yesterday after the close and some other near-term potential catalysts. The company will host a webcast at 11 am EST today, which can be watched here.

Integra Resources was also the largest position size in my portfolio during much of last year, but I did decrease my position substantially following the impressive rally last year, but I recently bought more shares after the significant selloff.

Last year, Integra's stock price was a massive outperformer, but it has so far underperformed substantially this year and is down 33%. Most precious metals mining stocks have suffered lately, but softer Q4 2025 production numbers at Florida Canyon and a $62M bought deal during Q1 2026 haven't done wonders for the sentiment in this stock.

With that said, I remain bullish on Integra Resources, which has an extremely attractive valuation; there is expected resource and reserve growth at Florida Canyon during the summer, and the development project DeLamar is progressing towards a potential construction decision in 2028.

Q4 2025 Result

The Q4 production numbers for Integra Resources have been known for a while, where the gold production was only 12,864 ounces, which was a material decline compared to what we have seen at Florida Canyon lately. The drop in production was due to a liner tear in a solution pond, which has since been repaired. There were no solution releases and no environmental impact. So, part of the expected production in Q4 was deferred to 2026.

We also saw elevated cost levels in Q4 2025. The cash cost was $2,036/oz, and the mine-site all-in sustaining cost ("AISC") was as high as $3,371/oz in the most recent quarter. The higher costs were partly due to higher royalties from a higher gold price, but AISC was elevated due to a higher amount of equipment purchases during the quarter as well. However, due to a strong realized gold price of $4,229/oz in Q4, the company still operated with a decent margin.

The company still managed to meet its annual gold production guidance of 70-75 Koz in 2025, with 70,927 gold ounces produced for the full year. The cash cost was $1,937/oz and the mine-site AISC was $2,693/oz for the full year, which were slightly above the guidance ranges for 2025. This was primarily due to higher royalties from a higher gold price than budgeted for.

The gold production guidance for next year is expected to be about the same, at 70-75 Koz, and production will increase to around 85 Koz in the following years. 2026 is another year of elevated sustaining capital investments, which is why the mine-site AISC is expected to be as high as $2,850/oz, but the expectation is that AISC will decrease from 2027 and onwards. We will get more clarity on Florida Canyon in Q3 this year, when an updated technical report is expected to be released.

Integra saw softer earnings and operating cash flow from losses on the convertibles and an inventory build during Q4. However, adjusted operating cash flow was a healthy $21M, and adjusted earnings were $15M, not that far off from what we saw last quarter with significantly higher production. The high gold price offset some of the negative impact from the lower production.

Florida Canyon is funding much of the capital expenditures of Integra, but total capital expenditures are especially high in 2026 due to sustaining capex at Florida Canyon together with significant pre-production capex at both DeLamar and Nevada North. This explains why the company closed a $62M bought deal earlier this quarter.

The last of the convertibles were converted in Q4 2025, which means Integra is debt-free and had a cash position of $63M at the end of Q4, but it comes to $115M if we include the recent bought deal.

Valuation

Integra is a bit of a mix of a development company and a producer. The production at Florida Canyon is meaningful under the current gold price environment, but the company still gets most of its value from the development projects.

Using the latest share price, the latest fully diluted share count, and a net cash position of $115M, Integra has a market cap of $581M and an enterprise value of $466M.

According to a recent feasibility study, DeLamar alone has a net present value ("NPV") estimate of $1.5B, using a $4,000/oz gold price assumption and a 5% discount rate. I estimate the total NPV for Integra's assets to be around $3B, with the same assumptions. We should get more precise estimates on Florida Canyon later this year with an updated mine plan. Regardless, the EV to NPV for Integra comes to around 0.16, which is an extremely attractive valuation.

In 2027, Florida Canyon alone might be producing around 85Koz of gold with an AISC of around $2,500/oz. If we use the same gold price assumption as above, the sustaining free cash flow might be around $100M in 2027. That translates to a sustaining free cash flow yield of around 21%. So, I think it is fair to describe Integra as very cheap, both based on its total assets and near-term cash flow generation potential.

Conclusion

Integra Resources was one of my largest positions going into 2025, which, combined with the stellar stock price performance, led to a portfolio contribution of 31% from this stock in 2025. That naturally led to an outsized position size towards the end of last year, and I substantially decreased my holdings in Integra. Following the recent stock price decline, I have started to buy back some of the shares I sold, and I think this stock offers an excellent risk-reward here.

The company has production that should generate solid cash flow going forward, the balance sheet is strong, and all its assets are in the United States. There is also a clear plan for permitting the flagship DeLamar development project. This year, the main catalyst is probably an updated technical report on Florida Canyon, which has the potential to materially extend the mine life of that asset. That, combined with lower sustaining capex going forward, makes Florida Canyon a key component of this company.

I rate Integra Resources as a strong buy at this level, and the main risk is a sharp decline in the gold price from the current level, which is not my base case going forward. There are also permitting and execution risks over the next few years to consider.

data.title: Filtering The Noise - Protect Your Income Stream
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving.

There is a lot of noise in the market. On any given morning, you will wake up to headlines blaring about the price of oil, the dangers of private credit, AI being overpriced, AI being underpriced, the economy being weak, the economy recovering, the market rising, or the market falling.

Historically, the markets have a tendency to overreact to noise. They get overly enthusiastic about good things like the dot-com boom. They get overly pessimistic about dangers. Often overshooting to the upside and the downside.

The noise never really goes away; it just changes. Let's take a look at some noise the markets made in prior years, just in the month of March.

March 2023: Banks Are Failing!

How many of you remember three years ago? SVB Financial (SIVBQ) failed, and pundits were on TV talking about the "contagion" that was going to spread throughout the banking sector, stoking fears of runs on banks. Many retail investors were learning for the first time that banks were sitting on billions of unrealized losses in their HTM (hold-to-maturity) portfolios. Suddenly, bears were coming out of the woodwork and pointing to these as some sort of huge systemic risk.

Our immediate response to the hype against banks was to double down on buying banks, writing:

Banks sold off heavily on Friday, March 10th, and BTO was down 5.6% (plus another 2% because it was ex-div). As I outlined to our subscribers, I do not see that what happened at SI and SIVB as a systematic risk throughout the banking sector. On average, banks are very strong and are in a far superior financial position than they were during the GFC.

The reality is that when you are dealing with "averages," there will be some holdings that are below average. Even when a sector is strong, there will always be some outliers that will fail. We are encouraged that BTO avoided two banks that turned out to be much weaker than average. While others are dumping all the good banks, we are happy to increase our exposure to the sector through BTO."

Fast forward three years later, and BTO is paying the same distribution and trading in the same price ballpark it was trading at before most investors were even aware that there was a bank named Silicon Valley Bank.

March 2020: COVID Is Different!

Three years before that, you all know what happened. The market was panicking. While some talking heads were suggesting that investors should sell everything, Bill Ackman did a TV interview on Wednesday, March 18th, where he said, "Hell is coming" and warned that major U.S. businesses were going to fail.

Meanwhile, we were furiously publishing articles encouraging investors to keep calm and to take advantage of the buying opportunities.

You can go read the comments of those articles-they all received hundreds of them, with a very large proportion of those comments declaring that things would get worse, that our picks were too risky, etc. In hindsight, it was the bottom for the market and for our portfolio.

We didn't know it was the bottom at the time. We didn't know how bad things were going to get, and things did get worse. Yet, our response was to meet the panic with calmness.

Approach Your Investments Like a Professional

Go sit in an emergency room and watch how the doctors handle patients who are coming in. Are they yelling and screaming in a panic? Of course not. The stakes are high, people's lives are in danger, and some of them will die. Yet, an ER doctor approaches the situation in a methodical manner. They use their training and experience to focus on the patient at hand in a methodical manner. They don't run around screaming, and they aren't getting distracted by whatever is happening in the next room. They remain calm, they go through the standard of care, and they act in a methodical manner to provide this patient the best opportunity. They cut through the noise and focus on the factors that really matter.

As investors, this is the mentality we should take when approaching the market. Reacting to the noise created by the news is going to be detrimental to your portfolio. It's not because the news is necessarily "wrong" - banks did fail in 2023, and in 2020, a lot of businesses were shut down for a long time. Dividends were cut, some companies went out of business, and many companies had lower profits for a few years. In both scenarios, there were real issues. Yet, panicking and selling all banks in 2023 was the wrong response. Similarly, selling everything in 2020 was the wrong response.

How do you find that calm to approach the markets deliberately and methodically?

Know Your Focus

At High Dividend Opportunities, our focus is income. Our goal is to generate a high level of dividend and interest income and to grow that income every year. As a result, our decisions are made in light of whether they bring us closer or further away from that goal.

Other investors approach the market with a different focus. That's fine. The U.S. stock market has been one of the greatest generators of wealth in the history of the world. There are many ways to make money from the stock market. Flailing about buying and selling because you saw a news article that this sector is great and this sector is in trouble isn't one of them. You can search the internet right now and find a bullish take and a bearish take on almost every ticker that is traded in the market.

Know what you are trying to achieve with your portfolio and know what your strategy is.

Set Your Safeguards

A plan that requires everything to go great is a bad plan. Things will go wrong; that's just life. The problem is that when the unexpected happens, it is not the right time to be coming up with a plan. The best plans are developed when we are in a calm and stable environment and can think through our options.

Since we are pursuing an income-centric strategy, our safeguards are going to center around protecting our income streams. We do this in three primary ways:

  1. We have a cushion. We plan on withdrawing a maximum of 75% of our cash flow. While the primary goal is to reinvest the extra 25% for future growth, there is also a benefit that when there is a period with elevated dividend cuts, like COVID, we have a cushion.
  2. We diversify across a minimum of 42 different investments. This way, no one investment is going to account for an overly large portion of our cash flow. If you hold two stocks, and one of them cuts the dividend by 50%, that is devastating. If you hold 42 investments and 10 of them cut their dividends by 50%-well, that is a 10-15% decline in your overall dividend income.
  3. Dedicate a portion of your income to fixed-income investments. While fixed-income investments can suspend dividends, and sometimes there are defaults, overall, the cash flow is more stable than common equities.

This all works together. We have a safety cushion, we have diversification so that we always have some holdings that are increasing their dividends and are in sectors relatively less impacted, and we have a conservative portion of our portfolio that will have less income volatility. Three distinct safety measures that help us ensure that even in the midst of change, our cash flow will continue to exceed the amount we intend to withdraw.

Slow Down and Consider Your Options

The best decisions are not rushed decisions. There is a lot of noise encouraging you to "do something" quickly, whether that is to buy more or to sell. The reality is that investing is a long-term endeavor. You don't need to make a decision to do anything on March 17th, 2020. There is plenty of time to step back, take a breather, and assess the situation. In fact, a very large number of people would have dramatically higher returns in their portfolio if they were on vacation the week of March 17th and they didn't come back until April.

You don't have to buy or sell "right now." Make sure you are stepping back and analyzing the situation with a clear head. Don't buy into the media hype. Not because the media is "wrong"-there is often a kernel of truth behind what is reported-but because hyperbole gets views.

This is why we suggested having a 5-step checklist that we discussed last week.

Current Fears Impacting Our Portfolio

There is no secret which part of our portfolio is being impacted the hardest by the current fear cycle. It is BDCs and CLOs, which are actually very different investments in very different types of loans but are being lumped together in the narrative surrounding private credit.

We discussed that narrative as it relates to OBDC, where the management of Blue Owl (OWL) has been ground zero for the media coverage. The market is bearish on credit risk.

This narrative is creating some great buying opportunities, especially among BDCs that have historically had well below average default rates, like Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) and Ares Capital (ARCC).

And among the "stick your hand in the fire" type of investments, like CLO equity, which is trading below COVID-level valuations. As we discussed in recent earnings updates, we do not expect relief in CLOs to happen soon. Market sentiment is extremely bearish, and there is no obvious catalyst to turn that around. For at least another three months, it is very likely that bearishness will continue.

We don't know when sentiment will improve. This portion of our portfolio is in the red, and we expect it will stay there until sentiment shifts. Meanwhile, other portions of our portfolio that are focused on energy and infrastructure are well into the green.

That's the power of having a diversified portfolio, where you are generating income from a diverse array of businesses in diverse industries.

Conclusion

Our goal is to have a rational approach to achieving our goals. The Income Method isn't designed to "beat the market," whatever that means. Unless you are selling everything and leaving the market forever, you can't really declare that you "beat" it.

Our goal is to develop a stream of cash flow and approach the risks and challenges given to us along the way in a calm and rational manner. Problems will arise, but we have a principled framework to use to solve them and make sure that we achieve our goals.

Whether you choose to follow an income-based strategy, as we do, or a trading strategy to harvest capital gains, having a disciplined and methodical approach will help you achieve better results. Filter out the noise and remain focused on your goals.

data.title: The Current Market Rotation - One Of The Biggest Disruptions In Generations
data.description:
Know what you own, and know why you own it. - Peter Lynch

Introduction

Today's article, like many in recent months, is about the rotation. Initially, the rotation was mainly based on higher inflation and broadening growth expectations, something I reiterated in a recent article titled "The Market Has No Idea How Bullish This 'Run-It-Hot' Shift Is."

As always, the market is focused on near-term risks. Currently, that includes the war in Iran.

However, by doing so, it's missing the bigger picture, which I consider to be very bullish. We're seeing strong efforts to kick-start accelerating economic growth in areas like housing, manufacturing, and related industries.

All of this is supported by rapid AI developments that I believe will help America reindustrialize its economy in a way we haven't seen in generations (yes, really).

Today, I will also talk about the rotation, economic growth, and AI disruption. However, we'll go a few steps further, as there's a lot more to it, including the "long-duration" versus the "short-duration" theme. That's what I have been working on and what I will share with you now.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get to it!

This Market Is Super Tough

While I am writing this, the S&P 500 is 7% below its 52-week high and down 5% on a year-to-date basis. That's not nice, but it's quite good compared to the fear that has been going around lately, all triggered by Iran-related stagflation fears. Also, note that because of fast-moving headlines, the picture may look different by the time you read this. But that's OK, as this article isn't about short-term developments.

However, as we all know, the market is top-heavy. The biggest 10 holdings (mostly FANG+ stocks) account for close to 40% of its weighting. That heavily distorts the picture. After doing some digging, I found some interesting numbers:

  • 57 S&P 500 stocks are down more than 20% year-to-date. That's 11% of the market (57/500*100).
  • 101 stocks are down 15% or more. That's one in five stocks.

That's also reflected in sentiment. Using AAII numbers, 52% of investors are bearish. That's 21 points above the long-term average. That's a lot, given that the market isn't even in a correction yet. But then again, it confirms market distortion. When many of your own stocks are down, you don't really care what the market is doing. Odds are you're pessimistic.

Needless to say, this weakness didn't start when the United States began its Iran operations. It started way earlier when AI disruption fears hit software. It even hit some of the market's favorite stocks, so to speak, as Microsoft (MSFT) peaked in 4Q25 at roughly $540. Now, you have to pay just $382 to get a share. It now trades at just 23.9x, which is almost in line with the S&P 500. That's how fast the market prices in new risks (and opportunities).

Microsoft is cheap for a reason, as the market is undergoing a somewhat "weird" transformation. The same goes for the economy, as we're in a "jobless boom." As we can see below, the U.S. economy has created no jobs over the past 12 months. Meanwhile, GDP growth has accelerated. This divergence is unique.

To visualize this relationship, I built the chart below, which shows the difference between total nonfarm payroll employment growth and nominal GDP growth. Generally speaking, that number is always negative, as technology allows the U.S. economy to grow output more than labor demand. That's good, as it indicates efficiency gains. However, since the pandemic, that relationship has been worse than usual. Between 2009 and 2020, economic growth was rather bullish for labor. Now, that isn't really the case anymore.

I could make this part alone a 2,000-word article, yet my point is that the market is nervous. Even before Iran, the market was super complex, as the old market we grew up in doesn't exist anymore. And yes, I am fully aware of how cheesy that sounds and that I'm 30 instead of 60 or 70. Many of you were already seasoned investors when I was learning how to read.

To me, this is the kind of market where stock picking isn't just handy to improve one's risk/reward, especially in light of the unfavorable valuation, but almost a must due to disruption risks.

This is the point where I talk about duration moats and risks.

We Need To Talk Way More About 'Long Duration'

I have to admit that I always feel a bit fancy when I talk about "long duration" assets. The definition, however, is quite straightforward (it's far from fancy). Long-duration assets are basically stocks from companies that are sensitive to interest rates because the majority of their cash flows are expected to be realized many years from now.

This includes growth companies with ultra-high valuations. These high valuations aren't dealbreakers if we assume growth is expected to remain high. Palantir (PLTR) comes to mind. In 2021, it generated $0.13 in EPS. This year, it's expected to be $1.30 (10x the 2021 result). It still trades at more than 170x earnings, but if it maintains elevated, double-digit EPS growth for years to come, it may not be expensive.

Needless to say, this isn't about PLTR. It's just an example. Regardless of what you think of this stock, I think we can all agree that it's a long-duration asset.

It's also one of the reasons why the ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK), which owns a lot of growth stocks, did so well in 2020 and 2021 and so poorly in the years after that, as rising rates and inflation made it less attractive to own growth stocks. After all, if the majority of a company's earnings are generated many years from now, higher inflation/rates require investors to apply a higher discount rate in their valuation. That's the theory in a nutshell.

That definition of "duration" is still valid.

However, we need to add something to it. Because of AI, technology disruption risks have exploded. That's a huge factor for duration. After all, if you have to hold an asset for many years until it starts to return cash, you are subject to many years of what could be severe disruption risks.

Before the Iran War, the market was entirely focused on this disruption. It's why software and even giants like Microsoft have performed so poorly. Whether it's Microsoft, Adobe (ADBE), or ServiceNow (NOW), these stocks all have two things in common:

  • They are still expected to see high EPS growth for years to come.
  • The market isn't willing to pay the kind of premiums it paid before 2026.

That's the textbook example of duration risk.

Initially, AI was nothing more than a smart search engine. Now, it's different. It feels like AI went from doing students' homework to taking their jobs. Now, we're in the age of agentic AI, where bots are starting to work (semi-) autonomously.

Here are a few short quotes from a recent Wall Street Journal article on this subject:

The increasing sophistication of AI tools has reshaped what it means to be a software engineer in this town. The best developers aren't writing code anymore; they're learning how to lead a small army of AI assistants.

[...] On a February podcast, Boris Cherny, who leads Claude Code, declared that "the title software engineer is going to start to go away." During the recording, he had five agents working in the background.

An Andreessen Horowitz partner joked on X that future generations will "grow up in a world where B.C. refers to "Before Claude."

[...] Jeff Seibert, founder and CEO of AI accounting startup Digits, said he hasn't written code since December. Seibert's new preferred method of working: dictating all of his directions to Claude Code. - WSJ

One person I am very close with ran a very successful software company in a specific niche that he sold to a serial acquirer in 2018. I asked him the other day if he thinks it's good or bad that AI became a thing after he retired. He's glad that AI wasn't a thing back then, as he knows he would have been unable to create a competitive edge.

While I have often written that many software companies will be just fine because they'll adopt AI and become more efficient, my main thesis is that the entire industry will be disrupted. Even if large players can survive, their competitive moats are weakened. If you know any software developers, spend a few minutes with them. They will tell you just how much more efficient they are. Many can build websites within days. However, so can everyone else.

Suddenly, the value of coding has imploded.

The same goes for college graduates. As the price of knowledge is dropping, you can imagine that people are freaking out. Ever since AI became mainstream, I have worked overtime to figure out how I can stay ahead of the trend and remain relevant. It's one of the reasons why I am investing even more in stocks, as I know I need a large nest egg.

Anyway, before I get completely off track with my personal stories, I thought about this a bit more. There are three sectors in the S&P 500 that are basically long-duration assets.

  • Information technology. This is the easiest choice, as it covers cloud computing, software, and related applications.
  • Consumer discretionary. Technically speaking, consumer stocks aren't long-duration. However, a closer look at its holdings shows that roughly 40% of the sector consists of Amazon (AMZN) and Tesla (TSLA), which are both "tech" stocks.
  • Communication services. This sector is also dominated by two companies. These are Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL). Alphabet basically owns the internet (I say that jokingly, as they have so many services), while Meta is the leader in social media. Both are tech stocks.

With that in mind, looking at the market's year-to-date performance (see below), we see that all of these sectors are down. They are three of the five sectors with a negative performance.

While the Iran War is obviously a driver of negative returns, underperformance is mostly caused by disruption fears, in my honest opinion. And although I think some companies will bounce back in a meaningful way, I do not see a path to the elevated returns of the past.

You may obviously disagree with me (I even encourage it), but I think the age of software is over.

And even financials are pressured by these risks, as private credit spent more than a decade aggressively investing and lending to software companies, as I wrote in this article.

The Blackstone Private Credit Fund is a good example of this. Although I am not saying it has risky loans (it's just an example), it shows more than a quarter of its loans are in software. It also has 11% professional services exposure.

These loans may end up doing just fine, yet they show the elevated exposure. The market is currently trying to make sense of that and assess the new risk/reward. Moreover, financials are now suffering from rising rates and growth uncertainty. I think some select companies in this space offer a great investment opportunity, but for now, it is what it is.

To make matters worse, when we add that life insurers, multi-strategy funds, pension funds, and other giants are the main investors in these assets, you can imagine that some tend to get nervous in this market. Again, I'm not saying this is 2007/2008 all over again.

I am saying we're in a regime change, and long-duration assets need to be re-evaluated.

The first two months of this year were all about that, and I don't think we're finished yet.

Short-Duration Is Where It's At

What's the opposite of long duration? It's mostly value companies that do not depend on long-term growth stories. This includes commodity companies, industrials, materials, financials, consumer staples, utilities, and real estate. In other words, the sectors that are currently leading the S&P 500.

Energy is, by far, the leader, as it has two tailwinds:

  • In January and February, it benefited from rising growth expectations and the fact that the sector was extremely cheap to begin with.
  • Although the Iran War has hurt growth expectations, the market has realized that energy not only solves the AI power bottleneck but also benefits from prolonged supply headwinds, including from the bombing of strategic assets in the Middle East.

Don't get me wrong. Currently, I am not chasing energy, as I fully expect a steep pullback in the event of good Iran headlines. However, structurally, I think the market has woken up to the fact that economic growth is broadening and that short-duration companies not only provide more AI disruption resistance but also fuel and support the AI revolution.

It's a rotation I expect to last, especially if inflation remains higher for longer.

The only problem (a pretty big problem) is that the war in Iran pressures economic growth expectations. The higher the risk of a recession, the lower the interest rate of the market to buy value stocks, except for some very safe utilities or consumer staples, maybe.

That's why I expect a repeat of the January/February momentum the moment recession risks start to fade again.

Thoughts on Positioning and Takeaway

I spent hours preparing every single article, but today, I once again realized that my many segues made this article quite long. So, before I lose you, here's my strategy on positioning.

  • I remain bullish on the S&P 500, although I believe we have to diversify to achieve decent long-term returns.
  • For that, I think three things make sense to keep in mind.
  1. I believe hard assets and short-duration sectors are the best place to be in the years ahead. Think of energy stocks, materials, and industrials (i.e., transportation, machinery, infrastructure, etc.). This also includes miners, utilities, and a few select consumer staples with pricing power.
  2. If you like some software stocks, that's fine. However, my main belief is that we should be underweight in this sector, regardless of how attractive some software stocks may look at current prices.
  3. Tactical investments for specific tailwinds can add substantial alpha over the years ahead. In my case, I'm likely buying some gold miners/streamers soon, more landowners like LandBridge (LB) to benefit from the data center buildout, and railroads like Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP) for economic re-shoring tailwinds.

All things considered, we're not dealing with a typical market cycle. This is a structural shift that's currently masked a bit by the war in Iran. The old playbook ended going into this year; at least, that's when it became a bit more obvious.

As a result, I think we should reassess our strategies. While I think the S&P 500 itself will be fine, we need to dive deeper to find risks and opportunities that impact our portfolios and strategies. For me, a big part of that is shifting from long duration to short duration.

Needless to say, I'll keep you up to date and provide a lot more ideas.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: What's Gotten Into Gold? My Three Answers
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Gold is the ultimate hedge against global chaos. Or at least, that’s what it has been historically. But during this Middle East crisis, it has been anything but rock-solid. The question is... why?

Check out the MoneyShow Chart of the Day, a weekly chart of the SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) going back a half decade. You can see the incredibly powerful bull market of the last few years... how wildly overbought GLD got several weeks ago... and how the ETF has pulled back dramatically from its late-January high.

Gold: Why Isn't it Thriving Here?

(Source: StockCharts)

Moreover, that decline accelerated after hostilities broke out in late February. Gold plunged 15% - losing value for a nine-day stretch at one point - after the bombs started falling in the Middle East.

So, what’s gotten into gold? Here are my three answers...

1. The Technical Blow-Off. Gold rose incredibly far, incredibly fast in late 2025. Then it experienced a wild, exuberant blow-off top in January. I don’t care what asset you’re talking about. When you get that powerful a move, with that powerful a technical blow-off, you’re going to get some ugly action afterward. Not to mention a lengthy period of consolidation.

2. The Radical Rate Reversal. It wasn’t that long ago we were talking about more Federal Reserve rate cuts in 2026. Now, markets are starting to price in potential rate hikes. And this isn’t a US-only thing. It’s happening in several global markets, too.

Long-term yields have surged as well. Investors are worried that higher energy prices will fuel more inflation - while spiraling war-related costs will necessitate more government bond issuance. Since gold yields nothing, higher yields on other assets can increase competition for funds. That puts downward pressure on gold.

3. The Scramble for Cash. Look at all markets in March. Stocks have been selling off. Bonds have been selling off. Gold has been selling off. That smacks of derisking and deleveraging. Or, in simple terms, a scramble to raise cash. Some central banks (like Turkey) are also reportedly considering liquidating gold holdings to bolster their cash reserves.

If you’re asking why your primary safe haven asset isn’t keeping you safe from losses, those are my three answers.

But I also believe the long-term bull market remains intact. The forces that helped push gold to $3,000, then $4,000, then (briefly) $5,000 still look to be in place. So, my advice is simple. Maybe don’t be in such a hurry to get rid of your gold.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Six Figure Limit: The CRFB proposes capping Social Security benefits at $100K for wealthy couples.

Deal, or no deal?

The stock market is reacting positively to President Donald Trump's claim that Iran wants a deal to end the war, which is now in its fourth week. Even crude oil futures are down, signaling optimism over the Trump administration's eagerness to find an off-ramp from the conflict. But is there an end in sight?

Washington's view: The U.S. reportedly sent Iran a 15-point plan to end the war, delivered through Pakistani intermediaries. The plan addresses Iran's ballistic missile and nuclear programs, as well as maritime routes through the Strait of Hormuz, which Tehran has effectively blocked. It remains unclear whether Israel, which has been bombing Iran alongside the U.S., is on board with the proposal. Trump also announced that Iran offered the U.S. a "present" that's "worth a tremendous amount of money" as a show of good faith amid negotiations. He said the gift was related to energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

Tehran's response: But Iran has refuted Trump's claims. "Has the level of your inner struggle reached ‌the ⁠stage of you negotiating with yourself?" Ebrahim Zolfaghari, spokesperson for the Khatam al-Anbiya ​Central Headquarters (Iran's main military command), asked. "You will see neither your investments in the region nor the former prices of energy and oil again, until you understand that stability in the region is guaranteed by the powerful hand of our armed forces." Tehran has reportedly set a high bar for ceasefire negotiations, demanding that the U.S. shut down its Gulf bases and pay reparations for its attacks. It also wants to collect fees from ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz and keep its missile program with no negotiations to limit it, among other demands.

Bigger picture: "Markets desperately want to believe in the positive," UBS' Paul Donovan noted. "Focus on the apparent 15-point U.S. plan to end the war has received more attention than Iranian dismissals of this, or the fact that passage through the Strait of Hormuz is minimal." SA analyst Eugenio Catone on Monday said he remained cautious despite Trump's de-escalation claims. "At this point, I believe that both Iran and Israel are the main actors in this war, so they are the most reliable sources to understand where this conflict is really heading," he said. "Right now, none of them is stepping back; therefore, I consider the recent stock market enthusiasm as a dead cat bounce."
