data.title: Cosan's Deleveraging Story Still Lacks A Solid Foundation
data.description:
Revisiting the Bear Case

The last time I wrote about Cosan, I had summarized the bearish thesis as follows:

“The company's thesis has been based on high leverage, since high-interest expenses accumulate as a result of the very high-interest rate scenario in Brazil, and dividend generation has been on the verge of insufficiency. Thus, Cosan is forced to divest to strengthen its cash position, something I do not see as healthy or sustainable for a holding company.”

To make it clear, Cosan is a conglomerate of companies (Rumo (RUMOF), Compass, Moove, Radar, and Raízen) that basically lives off dividends and the appreciation of the equity of its holdings. The problem with the thesis is that Cosan has become an overly complex structure, mixing several cyclical and cash-intensive segments (sugar, ethanol, fuels), causing the market to apply a "conglomerate discount" to the thesis.

The Numbers Don’t Tell a Clean Story

The latest earnings print (4Q25 and FY25) highlighted some unresolved issues at Cosan in my view. Deleveraging improved, with a 3.3x pro-forma net debt-to-EBITDA ratio vs. 3.7x in 3Q25, with expanded net debt falling by 58% YoY despite the fact that dividends received also fell by 41% in the year, with an interest coverage of 0.9x—showing that Cosan still does not cover its interest expenses with its own cash flow.

The biggest problem, as I see it, is that this debt reduction did not come organically. Cosan carried out a capital increase of R$10.5 billion, diluting its shareholders, and sold core assets (stake in Vale (VALE)). So, this "improvement" in Cosan's reported results came much more from balance sheet engineering than via cash generation.

The structural risk for the holding company remains very high, as I view it, since with Cosan not fully controlling the flow, the dependence of Raízen, Rumo, and Compass on allocating their capital and eventually entering a bad cycle leaves Cosan "in the lurch", with dividends being reduced.

Turning to Raízen, this is the "elephant in the room". In early March, Raízen—which practically plants and processes sugarcane and transforms it into sugar and biofuel, but also distributes fuels (Shell in Brazil)—filed for out-of-court reorganization, with a debt of R$65 billion (the largest out-of-court reorganization case in Brazil).

About ~50% of creditors agreed to negotiate, but the real risk of this process for Cosan is if there is a conversion of debt into shares, where Cosan's stake may fall. If Raízen was previously seen as Cosan's main growth asset (energy transition), it is fair to say that today it is the weakest spot for the thesis. Naturally, this tends to lengthen the dividend "drought" and also creates a need for additional capital injections.

Finally, I would also bring as negative highlights Cosan’s drop in consolidated EBITDA by -15% in FY25. Only Raízen added with a -25% YoY drop in EBITDA. Radar and Compass fell -6% and -1% YoY, respectively, and only Rumo managed to grow 4%. This shows how Cosan's scenario does not come from a single factor, but from a spread deterioration, which is why deleveraging cannot be organic. At the same time, the market is getting skeptical about Cosan’s capital allocation strategy in the face of the bad timing of past acquisitions (such as the acquisition of a stake in Vale), and this creates a perception of reactive and non-creative management.

It does little to help that the interest rate in Brazil (Selic) is at 14.75%—recently cut by 25 bps—putting pressure on debt rates. As the table below depicts, Cosan's FY25 financial result was -R$2.87 billion, even if it improved from -R$5.16 billion in 2024. Derivatives have improved quite a bit, but less mark-to-market and financial distortions still do not indicate structural improvement. If, in fact, the Selic enters a cycle of cuts (although I do not believe in an interest rate scenario below 14% this year), this could be a catalyst for CSAN, given its sensitivity to interest rates. But I reinforce that even with lower interest rates, it does not solve the structural problem that still needs to circumvent execution risks and possibly divest assets.

The Discount Is Not as Large as It Seems

Arguably, the best way to look at Cosan's valuation, as a holding company, is through a sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) analysis of its holdings and compare to its current market value.

Raízen, Rumo, and Comgás (which is part of Compass) are companies traded on the Brazilian stock exchange (B3), so here we can use their market cap as a proxy.

At the time of writing this paragraph, Raízen (in which Cosan has a 39% stake) has a market cap of BRL 5.7 billion. Rumo (where Cosan has a 30% stake) has a market cap of BRL 30.29 billion. And Comgás (where Cosan has an 88% stake indirectly), trades at BRL 16.63 billion, but here I will use an approximate market value of ~BRL 14.63 billion for Compass.

Now, on the non-listed side, I will use BRL 5 billion equity value for Moove and BRL 5 billion equity value for Radar in a more optimistic scenario. This would result in a market value of ~R$35.9 billion for Cosan, a holding discount of ~42%.

But that's the raw number. Factoring Cosan's net debt of R$9.7 billion into the calculation, adjusted SOTP would be ~R$26.2 billion, a holding discount of ~21%. I would say that, although relevant, this is not a huge discount.

Historically, holding companies in LATAM (such as Itaúsa) tend to trade in the 15–30% discount range depending on complexity, and I believe that CSAN likely deserves to trade toward the higher end of that range. This, in my view, weakens the argument that Cosan is trading at a steep discount in the face of structural headwinds and suggests that it may not be a clear mispricing in the way I understand it.

Final Remarks

I think it is not ideal that Cosan has become so event-driven. But ironically, that’s also the main risk to the bearish view.

Speculation is already circulating about potential sales of Cosan’s stake in Rumo (with the company already confirming that it is evaluating strategic alternatives). There is also a concrete IPO plan already filed by Compass that could be a "cleaner" way to deleverage. I would say that the management team’s explicit statement in the earnings call that "we are considering every single business as a potential target to be sold partially" reinforces that selling its assets to deleverage is very much on the table. This may well be a healthier outcome to put Cosan in a position where it can deleverage organically in the foreseeable future and be able to cover its interest expenses with its own cash flow.

But other than that, I remain pessimistic about Cosan's thesis, and I would not dare to touch it as long as there is a clear path of organic and sustainable deleveraging. As I see the thesis at the moment, the turning point goes beyond simply reducing debt and instead lies in the holding company's ability to sustain its own cash flows—i.e., when dividends consistently cover financial expenses again. Until then, any improvement that is not structural but cosmetic and leaves the thesis at the mercy of events is likely to result in limited asymmetry. That being said, I reiterate my bearish call on CSAN.

data.title: Kanzhun: Focus On The Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Kanzhun Ltd. (BZ) previously with a buy rating, as I believe the investments in AI are truly helping BZ to improve recruiters' ROI and job seekers' engagement. Although the share price has gone the wrong way vs. what I expected, I am still giving this a buy rating as the core business is still growing at a healthy pace, white-collar hiring is recovering, and the customer base is broadening across smaller enterprises and lower-tier cities. More importantly, AI-related revenue has reached meaningful scale and is growing faster than the rest of the platform.

4Q25 earnings (announced on 18th March)

BZ fundamental performance continues to be solid. In the most recent quarter, revenue was up double digits as well, at 14% y/y, driving total revenue to RMB2.08 billion. Revenue from online recruitment services to enterprise customers was the main driver here, up 14.6% y/y, with healthy paid enterprise customer growth. Average monthly active users for the quarter were also up ~10% y/y, from 52.7 million to 58 million. Revenue growth came with lower operating costs, resulting in EBIT growing by ~80% y/y to RMB686 million. On an adjusted basis, that figure was lower at ~37% y/y, but still strong. Adj. net income, as a result, grew 25.4% y/y to RMB905.9 million.

White-collar hiring is recovering

BZ's stock price decline is indeed worrisome, and I can get why the market is reacting that way. The bear case narratives go something like this: If white-collar demand stayed weak, or if AI ended up reducing hiring activity, then BZ could have ended up as a low-growth platform with some margin help but not much else. Apparently, this is not the case, and there are data to back this up. Management said white-collar recruitment demand recovered steadily in 2025, and after adjusting for Chinese New Year timing, average daily job postings in the 15 days after the holiday surged by a double-digit percentage y/y. Management also said manufacturing, electronics and communications, semiconductors, automotive, advertising and media all grew faster, while newly added and active job postings for TMT sectors accelerated significantly. Overall, white-collar job postings grew positively y/y.

Previously, for BZ, the equity story was largely anchored against blue-collar hiring, but with white-collar hiring also back in the game, this significantly improves the narrative since there are two growth drivers here. Based on the comments given, BZ is effectively telling us the recovery is already showing up in job posting activity, and importantly, it is coming from a broad range of industries, which suggests the demand is more durable.

Customer base and ARPPU

The customer base also widened for the better. Total paid enterprise customers went up ~12% y/y to a total of 6.83 million, and management said revenue contribution from third-tier and lower-tier cities was 25% in Q4. They also said revenue from enterprises with <100 employees went >50% for the first time in 2025. This is a pretty significant development, in my view, as a broader mix across city tiers and smaller businesses means BZ demand has become more resilient (now that it leans on a wider base of demand, not just a handful of large accounts or one industry).

Normally, this kind of mix shift toward SMEs should pressure ARPPU, since smaller customers tend to spend less, but the fact that ARPPU stayed broadly stable despite this suggests BZ's value proposition is stronger and/or more customers are spending more. Either way, it's a good outcome and sets up well for further ARPPU gains as BZ ramps up monetization efforts (more on this below on AI).

AI is becoming a real revenue driver

BZ's AI efforts are shaping up well. Please refer to my previous writeup for some examples of BZ's AI solutions, but a brief recap is that the AI solutions are enabling BZ to not just help employers and job seekers find each other. It is moving closer to the hiring process itself, which matters for the monetization story because the monetization potential is much larger than simply selling/routing traffic. If AI can help employers hire faster and with better results, BZ has a stronger case to capture more of the recruitment budget. And we know for a fact that this is happening, as revenue from closed-loop services had already reached the scale of hundreds of millions of RMB by FY2025 (per the transcript), and this business was growing faster than the rest of the platform.

In my view, that is the clearest proof that AI is becoming a real revenue driver. Previously, the AI narrative was more of a theory and lacked tangible revenue numbers for investors to be confident. But now, management is explicitly saying this business is already meaningful in size and growing faster than the core platform. I'd say this is a real revenue driver, and the market should attach value to it.

A capital return plan helps the stock

Finally, I think attention should be spent on the capital return story too. BZ's board has approved a plan to allocate no less than 50% of the previous year's adj. net income to dividends and share repurchases over the three-year period starting from 2026. They have also increased the repurchase program limit to US$400 million (this is ~6% yield). This itself is not a game changer to buy the stock, but I think it does provide support to the stock (i.e., a floor). Add on the core business getting better and the AI efforts showing traction; this capital return angle does make the overall story look better.

Valuation

I recognize that BZ's valuation has fallen off the cliff, from 16x NTM PE when I wrote about it previously to the current ~10.6x. My view is that the market is paying attention to the wrong parts of the business. Specifically, some market participants might have extrapolated management's Q1 2026 guide of high-single-digit growth for the full year, and because of this, believed that BZ's growth profile has structurally worsened. Some of them might also be worried about the pressure on margins as BZ puts in more AI investments. To me, all these are short-term views that don't truly reflect BZ's long-term growth profile. What readers should focus on is the fact that white-collar demand has improved for BZ and that AI is now a real revenue driver.

Relative to the peers, BZ now trades at its lowest premium vs. the average, which I don't think is fair given the growth profile. Consensus expects BZ to grow revenue by ~30+% over the next 24 months, and that is the highest within the peer group. Even PageGroup (MPGPF) which is only expected to grow by ~4%, is trading at 15x. This valuation discount should close as BZ executes and grows as expected, and if we see a re-rate back to 16x, that is almost a 50% upside.

Risk

If hiring demand softens again after the post-CNY strength, it could really weigh on investors' confidence as they start to believe growth is going to slow for the rest of the year. The investments needed to ramp BZ's AI efforts also come at a cost, and depending on how fast BZ is able to ramp up monetization here, the step up in cost could eat into margins and weigh on earnings.

Conclusion

Despite the share price performance, I give BZ a buy rating. I think the market is too focused on near-term noise such as softer Q1 guided growth and the cost of AI investment, while missing the bigger story. White-collar hiring has improved, the core platform is still delivering solid growth, and AI is starting to show up as a real revenue driver. With the stock now trading at a much lower multiple than before, I believe the current setup still offers attractive upside if execution stays on track.

data.title: Darden: Decelerating Comps And Inflation Pressures
data.description:
So far in 2026, investors are focused on the macro story: a war in Iran, AI disruption, skyrocketing oil prices, and a shaky U.S. consumer. Very few companies, especially those in sectors exposed to consumer spending like the restaurant sector, have been able to escape the broad-based pessimism in the markets.

Darden Restaurants, Inc. (DRI), however, is one major outlier. The parent company behind Olive Garden and Longhorn Steakhouse has seen its share price rise modestly this year, defying weakness elsewhere in the sector (especially among fast-casual restaurants). Can Darden truly justify this outperformance?

I last wrote a “Sell” article on Darden in November, when the stock was trading around $180 per share. Since then, a number of factors have moved against the stock's favor even further. Share prices have increased (even while many restaurant peers have floundered), making its relative valuation less appealing. Same-restaurant sales growth has also compressed, calling into question Darden's enriched valuation multiples. At the same time, the company is also facing some pressure from commodity inflation, which makes us uncertain about its ability to generate meaningful earnings growth in the year ahead. With all of this in mind, I'm reiterating my “Sell” rating on Darden.

Let's touch briefly first on commodities inflation. For the near term, the company is expecting high single-digit inflation in beef, which represents 25% of Darden's food basket, and double-digit inflation in seafood, which is 9%. “Other” costs (27% of spending), which may include paper and packaging, are also expected to rise in the mid-single digits, while the company is expecting inflation in all other categories except dairy and oil, which are expected to reduce in price.

Yes, Darden has hedges in place, and it notes it is ~80% covered against price movements. And yet we note that the company's margins have been bleeding over the past few quarters despite the high degree of coverage. At the same time, the Darden brands that are doing the best in terms of same-restaurant sales are also showcasing the weaker segment margin profiles.

All of this leads up to the question: Can we justify Darden's valuation? The company has recently updated its fiscal FY26 outlook (the year that ends soon, in May 2026) to indicate 9.5% sales growth on 4.5% same-restaurant sales growth and 70 new openings (stronger than 7.5%-8.5% total growth, 2.5%-3.5% same-restaurant sales growth, and 65 openings previously), while slightly bumping up pro forma EPS expectations to $10.57-$10.67 (with $0.25 of expected benefit from a 53-week fiscal year this year).

For next year, FY27, Wall Street is expecting Darden to deliver $13.70 billion in revenue (+4% y/y revenue growth) and $11.38 in pro forma EPS, which is 7% earnings growth (or 10% excluding the benefit of the 53-week year in FY26).

This positions Darden's valuation multiples at:

  • 18.9x FY26 P/E.
  • 17.7x FY27 P/E.

In my view, it's difficult to justify a high-teens P/E multiple for a restaurant company with decelerating comp sales and potential margin challenges.

As shown above, Darden sits on the higher end of valuations in the restaurant sector, especially when compared to other full-service, sit-down chains like The Cheesecake Factory Incorporated (CAKE), which is just trading at ~14x forward earnings.

Continue to steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q3 recap

Let's now go through Darden's latest fiscal Q3 (February quarter) results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Darden's revenue grew 6% y/y to $3.34 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $3.33 billion (+5% y/y) by just a one-point margin. What we are wary of, however, is the slight deceleration in same-restaurant sales growth. As a reminder, this metric measures the revenue growth from existing restaurants, without including the contribution of newly opened or recently closed stores.

As shown above, total Darden brand same-restaurant sales grew 4.2% y/y, which decelerated 50 bps versus 4.7% total-company same-restaurant sales in Q2. Interestingly, LongHorn Steakhouse and the Fine Dining segment (which includes higher-end seafood chains like Capital Grille and Eddy V's) saw an improvement to 7.2% and 2.1% same-restaurant sales growth in Q4, respectively, which was a 170 bps and 270 bps sequential lift. That said, this improvement was more than offset by the largest brand, Olive Garden, showcasing a 270 bps sequential decline to just 3.2% same-restaurant sales growth.

The good news here is that Darden still believes it's outperforming its industry, which management says is rather flat on same-restaurant sales. Per CEO Ricardo Cardenas' remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

We've been consistently outperforming industry same-restaurant sales, and this quarter our gap widened as each of our 4 largest brands exceeded the industry by more than 400 basis points. All of our segments delivered positive same-restaurant sales as our restaurant teams continue to be brilliant with the basics, once again leading to impressive guest satisfaction scores [...]

Olive Garden delivered positive same-restaurant sales of 3.2% for the quarter, driven by strong operational execution even with 3 fewer weeks of price pointed promotions than last year. The restaurant teams are focused on ensuring every guest is offered a free refill on breadsticks and soup or salad. This led to a new all-time high guest satisfaction score for service and matched their all-time high for overall guest satisfaction. In January, Olive Garden completed the rollout of the lighter portion section of their menu, adding 7 more dishes under $15.

Here's the issue, however: the stronger-performing brands are those with the weaker margins. We note in particular that LongHorn Steakhouse, whose 7.2% comps growth carried Darden's quarter, fell to an 18.6% segment profit margin (-110 bps y/y) and has a 440 bps margin deficit to Olive Garden.

We can see the impact of higher beef prices eating into LongHorn's margin potential. The clear issue here: as Darden's sales mix tilts into these lower-margin businesses, overall company earnings growth will slow—even if same-restaurant sales growth is outmuscling peers.

As shown in the chart below, restaurant-level EBITDA (an industry measure that excludes corporate overhead spend) clocked in at $703.6 million, or a 21.0% margin that fell 30 bps y/y (with a -50 bps headwind from food and beverage inflation partially offset by favorable labor costs).

Again, we don't think necessarily that Darden is doing poorly, but its valuation multiples, which sit above other full-service chains like Cheesecake, already price the stock for perfection and put it at risk for decay.

Key takeaways

In my view, Darden's outperformance since the start of 2026 is a good chance to take gains off the table and invest in other names. A macro overhang sits ahead of us, especially as higher oil prices hit the pump: fewer consumers in the United States may be willing to go out, especially to higher-ticket restaurants like LongHorn Steakhouse. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: First Watch Might Still Be A Good Long-Term Investment
data.description:
Investors may still be underestimating First Watch (FWRG). My original buy call doesn’t look so good now. First Watch’s guidance was weaker than I expected. It looks like 2026 will be a tough year for restaurants. This modern diner might have to raise prices later this year even though it’s been trying to limit price hikes. But I think the long-term investment thesis for First Watch is still intact. This modern diner could still replace the old diners and eventually become a national diner chain. It’s still planning to build lots of new restaurants. I also think its current 2026 guidance is achievable. And based on that guidance, it looks undervalued right now.

First Watch ended the year with strong overall growth. It reported 20.2% revenue growth in Q4 2025. But it also reported -1.9% traffic. One of the reasons why I said First Watch was a buy last time was because its traffic rose in the third quarter, so that’s not a good sign. Maybe its strong Q3 results were an outlier after all. While First Watch still posted 3.1% same-store sales growth for the quarter, that’s because it raised its prices last year. Higher prices also helped First Watch strengthen its margins. The most important metric, restaurant-level operating margin, rose 20 basis points to 19% for the quarter.

Now, it looks like First Watch might also report negative traffic in 2026. It might not build as many new restaurants in 2026 either. While I think its high P/E could be misleading because of restaurant construction costs, it also has a forward P/E of 63. Investors might overlook First Watch’s high P/E ratio if it can keep growing rapidly. If its growth slows down, its bottom line will become more important. But in 2027, if the economy improves, First Watch could also post stronger comps and build a lot more restaurants. So, the first thing I’d consider is First Watch’s long-term growth potential.

First Watch Plans to Have 2,200 Restaurants Eventually

In its 2025 10-K, First Watch pointed out that it could eventually reach 2,200 locations. It had 633 restaurants at the end of 2025. Its long-term target is low double-digit location growth. If it can increase its location count by 10% per year, it would have 2,185 restaurants in 13 years. So, this could become a major nationwide restaurant chain in 2038. Right now, it’s still a regional one. First Watch currently has locations in 23 states. And I think it’s valid for First Watch’s stock to have a growth premium because of this long-term plan.

But First Watch also plans to expand at a slightly slower pace in 2026. If you use the midpoint of its guidance and subtract the 3 locations it’s closing, it’s planning to add 58 net new restaurants in 2026. That’s a 9% growth rate. It makes sense for First Watch to slow down its growth because of economic weakness right now. I think the restaurant could probably speed up its expansion pace again once the economy improves.

First Watch Is Also Buying Franchised Restaurants

Some of the other restaurants that I’m following right now, such as Papa John’s (PZZA), are refranchising company-owned restaurants to improve their margins. So, it’s interesting that First Watch is making the opposite move. It’s buying restaurants from its franchisees and plans to keep doing that. Company-owned restaurants often perform better because the company has direct control over them, but they also have lower margins because the company has to pay all of the operating expenses. It also sounds to me like Wall Street analysts may not like this strategy because they usually prefer asset-light companies.

First Watch bought 19 restaurants from its franchisees in 2025 and has the right to buy back another 12 restaurants in 2026. So, the company can add incremental revenue by buying back more restaurants in 2026. I’d estimate that First Watch might be able to raise its revenue by 1 percentage point in 2026 by buying back locations from franchise owners. And First Watch can also use this lever to maintain its revenue growth in future years when it gains the rights to buy back more restaurants. But it still expects its revenue growth to slow down a lot this year.

First Watch’s Guidance for 2026 Is Weaker Than Expected

In my previous article, I said I expected First Watch to post 20% revenue growth in 2026. Now, the company is guiding for 12% to 14% revenue growth instead. This explains why its stock dropped after its Q4 report, but I think its new guidance is still achievable. First Watch is going to get a 9% uplift from new locations, and it can get a 1% uplift from buying back restaurants. And it’s also guiding for 1% to 3% comps. First Watch could achieve that goal by raising its prices, although it might report negative full-year traffic as a result.

But one of the reasons why I said First Watch was a buy in my previous article was that its traffic was rising, and now it sounds like that might not happen in 2026. First Watch is still being cautious about price increases, but it might raise prices later in the year. Higher commodity costs might not be transitory after all. Of course, this also means that other diners will have to raise their menu prices because their ingredient costs went up. First Watch should still have more pricing power than other diners because it attracts wealthy consumers who want healthier food. But lower-income consumers might go to the grocery store instead.

First Watch still expects to perform better than other restaurants in 2026. On the call, First Watch CEO Christopher Tomasso said, “And then on the Black Box, to be more specific, in my commentary, I talked about their current projection for the year is roughly a 3% industry-wide same-restaurant traffic decline.” Of course, if restaurants all report lower traffic and higher expenses in 2026, they might all trade at lower multiples.

The Main Risk Factor Is First Watch’s Premium Business Model

The restaurant sector is highly promotional right now. Pizzerias are using deep discounts to eliminate their competitors. Like First Watch, fast-casual pizzerias also use better ingredients than their peers and charge higher prices. And like I’ve said in my other articles, these pizzerias are shutting down because they’re losing business to competitors with lower prices. The thesis for First Watch depends on wealthier customers paying up for better food. But some of its customers might still decide to trade down. And other customers might buy meals from grocery stores.

Updated Comparative Valuation for First Watch

Like I said before, investors are paying more attention to the bottom line now. But I still think investors are valuing this restaurant incorrectly. If you look at its P/S ratio, it’s trading at a huge discount. First Watch currently has a forward P/S ratio of 0.54. That’s far lower than other growth restaurants’ P/S multiples. On March 23, Cava (CAVA) had a forward P/S of 6.80. And Dutch Bros (BROS) had a forward P/S of 3.11. Darden (DRI) had a forward P/S of 1.77. And Sweetgreen (SG), which is trading at a discount to all of these chains, still had a higher forward P/S than First Watch at 0.88.

[object HTMLElement]

Updated First Watch Valuation for 2026

It sounds like First Watch is considering a price increase in the second half of 2026 now. So, with the benefit of higher prices, it could still post 3% full-year comps in 2026. And I also think it will get a 1% revenue boost from buying back restaurants. And, again, it’s going to increase its location count by 9%. That adds up to 13% revenue growth. And that’s also the midpoint of First Watch’s 2026 revenue growth guidance. So, I’d project that First Watch’s revenue will rise 13% from $1.223 billion to $1.382 billion.

First Watch also reported full-year adjusted EBITDA of $120.9 million in 2025, so its adjusted EBITDA margin was 9.9%. If its EBITDA margin stays the same, it would report $136.8 million in 2026. That’s near the middle of its guidance range, which is $132 to $140 million. It sounds like First Watch expects its margins to remain about the same in 2026. It might have to raise its prices in the second half of the year to do that, but that’s still doable. So, I’ll estimate First Watch’s 2026 earnings under the assumption that it will report $136.8 million in adjusted EBITDA.

First Watch reported $75 million in depreciation in 2025, up from $57.7 million in 2024. That’s an increase of $17.3 million. First Watch is still building restaurants, although its pace has slowed down a bit. So, I’ll be conservative and project that its depreciation expense will rise another $17.3 million to $92.3 million in 2026.

The company also paid $16.7 million in interest, up from $12.6 million in 2024. It’s taking out loans to build restaurants and will need to borrow more money in 2026. It only has $21.2 million in cash right now. Again, I’ll be conservative and project that its interest costs will rise another $4.1 million to $20.8 million. After subtracting $113.1 million in depreciation and interest costs from $136.8 million in adjusted EBITDA, I get $23.7 million in EBIT. First Watch paid a 37% tax rate last year. So, if its tax rate is the same in 2027, its income would be $14.9 million. With 62.8 million shares outstanding, that’s full-year 2026 EPS of $0.24.

With a 2026 P/E of 63, my target price would be $15.12. This company’s current price is $12.35 per share. My updated price target is 22% higher than its current price. So, I’d say this stock is still a buy. And I’m using conservative assumptions. First Watch’s depreciation and interest costs might not rise as much this year as they did last year because the company is not planning to add as many new restaurants. Wall Street analysts currently have a price target of $19.50 and a strong buy rating on First Watch’s stock, so they’re even more confident in this company.

Conclusion

First Watch expects to grow at a slower rate in 2026. Conditions don’t look great for the restaurant sector right now. But I still think First Watch’s current guidance for 2026 is achievable. And I think that First Watch should also trade at a high P/E multiple because of its long-term growth potential. Its growth could accelerate in 2027 as the economy recovers. And if I estimate its 2026 results based on this guidance and its current multiples, this stock looks undervalued. So, I think First Watch is still a buy.
